Now that we've set the stage with the contrast phenomenon, the first of the seven principles of persuasion that we'll talk about is reciprocity. The principle of reciprocity is based on the social rule that people should give back to others who have given to them. That's it. It sounds simple, but it's anything but simple to use optimally and ethically in practice, and it has far-reaching implications for how people treat each other. If someone invites you to a party, you feel obligated to invite them to one of yours. If someone sends you a holiday greetings card, you feel obligated to send one back. And if someone does you a favor, you owe them a favor. And how many of us have found ourselves buying cheese at a grocery store just because we were given a small piece on a toothpick by that nice sample lady? It doesn't feel right to eat her cheese and just give her back the toothpick, right? So we buy some of her cheese. From the time we were children, we are all taught to give back to others that we must not take without giving in return. Why? Because in all societies, the rule for reciprocation allows for mutually beneficial relationships to begin and to continue.
